entities.description: Abstract medieval work/version kept distinct from its physical witness, modern editions, and digital encodings. It is a Middle English prose translation and adaptation of the Visio Pauli Redaction IV tradition.

entities.label: Eugen Kölbing, Eine bisher unbekannte me. Version von Pauli Höllenfahrt (1896)
notes: Kölbing states that this Middle English prose version is printed here for the first time.
He described it in 1896 as the sixth known Middle English version; that historical count is not substituted for a current witness or version census.

Request the complete current physical object: covers or boards, spine where photographable, pastedowns, every flyleaf and blank, both sides of ff. 1-100, stubs or detached matter, and edges where offered; ask for an image inventory and explicit omissions.
Associate CME00151 separately with ff. 81r-85r; do not crop the whole-object request to the work portion.
The following Three Arrows text also begins on f. 85r, so the terminal leaf should be captured in full.

BL says original manuscripts require expert photography and large or difficult orders may be discussed individually. Request color explicitly, especially if any existing surrogate or microfilm would otherwise be supplied in black and white.
Supply of images and permission to reproduce or publish them are separate questions under the imaging terms.

reviewNotes: Middle English is the language of the medieval work. German belongs to Kölbing's modern introduction and commentary; Latin belongs to the related source version and comparative apparatus.
Kölbing is the 1896 editor and transcriber, not the medieval author, despite the U-M item-level author field inherited from the article citation.

The modern BL collection record and specialist catalogs date Add MS 10036 to the fifteenth century or c. 1400-1425. Kölbing's historical fourteenth-century description is retained as a conflict, not silently adopted.
Warwickshire is a scribal-dialect localization for the witness, not a place-of-composition claim.
The Internet Archive Englische Studien 22 PDF is a 1965 reprint scan. Original 1896 publication, 1965 reprint, and modern digital facsimile remain distinct entities.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00151. U-M encoded Eugen Kölbing's 1896 first printing of an anonymous Middle English prose Vision of St. Paul from British Library, Add MS 10036, ff. 81r-85r. The encoding includes the series and article headings, Kölbing's short German introduction, Middle English text on printed pp. 134-137 (source lines 1-101), six inline notes, and folio, line, and page milestones, but omits the substantial German Anmerkungen that continue through p. 140. Kölbing transcribed the manuscript directly and compared the English text with Visio Pauli Redaction IV as printed by Herman Brandes in 1885; Brandes's printing was a modern comparison source, not the unknown physical Latin exemplar used by the medieval translator. The available Internet Archive volume is a complete scan of Johnson's 1965 reprint of Englische Studien 22, not an original 1896 carrier, so the reprint and scan are modeled separately. Complete public carriers for Kölbing's article and both Brandes sources have been cached. No public full facsimile of Add MS 10036 was located as of 2026-07-14; current catalog, reading-room, enquiry, and paid whole-object reproduction routes are recorded instead.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: By the first quarter of the fifteenth century; first known attestation c. 1400-1425; first printed 1896
catalogSummary.region: England; the Add MS 10036 scribe is localized to Warwickshire, but the place of composition is unknown
catalogSummary.title: Vision of St. Paul

catalogingSubject.label: Vision of St. Paul
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English prose translation and adaptation of the Visio Pauli, represented by the version in British Library, Add MS 10036, ff. 81r-85r. The medieval work is kept distinct from its Latin source version, manuscript witness, Eugen Kölbing's 1896 first printing and German commentary, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML. CME00151 includes the series and article headings, Kölbing's three-paragraph introduction, edited Middle English text, six inline notes, and source milestones, but omits the substantial Anmerkungen on printed pp. 137-140.

notes: Modern editorial paratext, excluded from the intrinsic medieval-work extent count.
contentParts.summary: The Miscellen series heading, Kölbing's German article title, and three German paragraphs identifying the newly printed prose version, Add MS 10036, its relation to Brandes's fourth Latin version, dialect limitations, and the transcription conventions.

contentParts.summary: The complete represented Middle English prose narrative, from Paul and Michael's prayer through their survey of infernal punishments, the souls' petition, Christ's grant of respite, and the final departure.

notes: This part documents the source-edition representation and does not imply that the omitted Anmerkungen belong to the medieval work.
contentParts.summary: Six inline textual notes, folio and source-line milestones, and typographic expansion cues survive in CME00151; the article's substantial German Anmerkungen following line 101 are omitted.

assertion.notes: The witness supplies a terminus ante quem, not an exact composition date.

dateStatements.display: By the first quarter of the fifteenth century (terminus ante quem c. 1400-1425)

scope.description: Latest plausible date for the Middle English translation-and-adaptation event, based on the surviving witness.

assertion.notes: The modern specialist catalogs date the codex to the beginning or first quarter of the fifteenth century; the BL collection record gives the broader fifteenth century.

scope.description: Earliest presently verified surviving witness of this Middle English version.

assertion.notes: Kölbing explicitly calls the text printed here for the first time; the article is signed Breslau, October 1895, but appeared in the 1896 volume.

scope.description: First printing of the Add MS 10036 Middle English version.

extent.description: One medieval prose work, printed by Kölbing as source lines 1-101. CME00151 also represents the Miscellen series heading, Kölbing's article title, three German introductory P elements, six retained inline NOTE elements, four printed-page PB elements, nine folio milestones (81a-85a = ff. 81r-85r), and twenty source-line milestones. The substantial article notes after line 101 on printed pp. 137-140 are omitted.

formStatements.description: A prose otherworld vision recounting Saint Paul and Michael's survey of punishments in Hell and the damned souls' request for respite.

scope.description: Continuous Middle English prose narrative and dialogue.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML TEXT/@LANG also lists German because Kölbing's modern introduction and apparatus are represented. German is excluded from work-level discovery language; Latin belongs to the related source layer.

scope.description: Language of the medieval prose work.

notes: The preferred title follows the modern MWM/IPMEP catalog record; the witness rubric and DMBR short title remain searchable alternates.
Kölbing is editor and transcriber of the immediate source, not author of the medieval work, despite U-M's source-article author field.
German in CME00151 belongs to the modern introduction and apparatus, and Latin belongs to the related source version; work-level discovery language is Middle English only.
Warwickshire is scribal dialect evidence for Add MS 10036, not a place-of-composition claim.
The work is prose. Kölbing's source line numbers are editorial lineation and do not convert it into verse.
CME00151 represents the medieval text completely as printed on pp. 134-137 but represents the 1896 article only partially because the Anmerkungen on pp. 137-140 are omitted.
Brandes's 1885 Redaction IV is Kölbing's printed comparison source; it is not identified as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.
The following Three Arrows text begins on the same manuscript folio, f. 85r, so the witness boundary is shared rather than exclusive.

next_steps: Collate Add MS 10036's distinctive readings against current Visio Pauli recension scholarship and Latin witnesses rather than treating Brandes's 1885 printing as the medieval exemplar.
Search IPMEP and prose-vision bibliographies for additional witnesses or a more precise version classification.
Retain anonymous responsibility, England-only cultural origin, and an unknown physical exemplar until direct evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Who translated or adapted this Middle English version, where was it composed, and which physical Latin exemplar or recension-specific manuscript was used?

next_steps: Inspect a complete color facsimile of Add MS 10036, especially quire 10, f. 85r, and the transition into the Three Arrows item.
Consult the latest BL curatorial dating and any IPMEP descriptive entry.
Continue to prefer modern specialist dating and MWM's side-specific folio locus while preserving the older and abbreviated claims as evidence history.
openQuestions.question: How should Kölbing's fourteenth-century manuscript date and DMBR's abbreviated ff. 81-84 range be reconciled with modern dating to c. 1400-1425 and the exact ff. 81r-85r locus?

next_steps: Check current IPMEP, DIMEV, and Visio Pauli bibliographies under IPMEP 535, the rubric, incipit, and explicit.
Do not add a 'unique-witness' tag until a current census is verified.
openQuestions.question: Is Add MS 10036 the sole surviving witness of this exact Middle English prose version, rather than merely the only witness known to Kölbing in 1896?

notes: The cataloged manuscript's precise origin and provenance are unknown.

scope.description: Broad cultural origin of the Middle English prose version.

assertion.notes: The Manuscripts of the West Midlands record cites LALME grid 447 256 and LP 4063. Kölbing cautioned that the text does not preserve a uniform authorial dialect.

notes: Do not convert witness scribal dialect into a composition-place assertion.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of the Add MS 10036 witness, not reconstructed authorial dialect.

notes: No named medieval author or translator is supplied by the witness or the consulted scholarship.

scope.description: Convenience display responsibility for an anonymously transmitted Middle English version.

assertion.notes: Kölbing describes a fairly faithful translation and compares it with Brandes's printed Redaction IV; the physical Latin exemplar remains unknown.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the Latin Visio Pauli Redaction IV tradition.
Vernacular reshaping of a Latin otherworld-vision version.
Modern transcription, introduction, commentary, and editorial representation in the immediate source.
First printed transcription of Add MS 10036, ff. 81r-85r.
Comparison with the fourth Latin version printed by Herman Brandes in 1885.
The narrative concentrates on Hell and its torments while referring to souls' ascent to Heaven or Paradise.
Punishment is paired with Christ's grant of periodic respite after the souls appeal through Paul and Michael.
The souls' request for rest and Christ's grant of respite from Saturday at noon until sunrise on Monday.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose version of the Visio Pauli. Saint Paul and the archangel Michael pass through Hell, where they see souls punished for sexual sins, usury, pride and covetousness, religious negligence, unbelief, harm to others, and failure to repent. The damned ask Paul and Michael to intercede. Christ rebukes their wickedness in life but, through the prayer of Michael and Paul and for his Resurrection, grants them a recurring respite from Saturday at noon until sunrise on Monday. The work closes with Paul and Michael suddenly departing the place of sorrow.

This record describes the medieval Middle English prose work represented by Add MS 10036. The Latin Visio Pauli Redaction IV is a related source work. Kölbing's 1896 German introduction, transcription conventions, notes, and comparison with Brandes are modern edition layers described in the linked lineage manifest.

The current record uses the modern specialist witness date c. 1400-1425 rather than Kölbing's 1896 description of the manuscript as fourteenth century. Warwickshire identifies scribal dialect only. The DMBR's abbreviated folio range 81-84 is not substituted for the more precise ff. 81r-85r given by the MWM catalog, Kölbing, and CME folio milestones.

scope.description: Established modern title for the Middle English work represented by the Add MS 10036 version.

notes: The MWM web record normalizes the conjunction to 'and', reads 'pried', and displays the yogh in Myȝel as an unsupported question mark.

scope.description: Opening words as transcribed by Kölbing and encoded in CME00151.

titles.value: how fele sorwes þer ben in helle. And so sodeynliche þei wente fro þat place

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: By the first quarter of the fifteenth century; first known attestation c. 1400-1425; first printed 1896

publication.embeddedTitle: MISCELLEN. — I. EINE BISHER UNBEKANNTE ME. VERSION VON PAULI HÖLLENFAHRT.

work.regionDisplay: England; the Add MS 10036 scribe is localized to Warwickshire, but the place of composition is unknown
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose version of the Visio Pauli. Saint Paul and the archangel Michael pass through Hell, where they see souls punished for sexual sins, usury, pride and covetousness, religious negligence, unbelief, harm to others, and failure to repent. The damned ask Paul and Michael to intercede. Christ rebukes their wickedness in life but, through the prayer of Michael and Paul and for his Resurrection, grants them a recurring respite from Saturday at noon until sunrise on Monday. The work closes with Paul and Michael suddenly departing the place of sorrow.
